query: Define a mathematical model for the Answer Entitlement E.
addendum_text:
[Box A Application Gate]
## 0. Application Gate

This document is used only when MMV or the 120B execution system is performing mathematical modeling, formalization, theoretical explanation, evaluation-formula design, paper or patent documentation, or design review of MMV internal behavior.

Do not apply this document as CRITERIA in the following cases:

- General questions.
- Questions that only require a short answer.
- Ordinary coding questions.
- Questions requiring up-to-date information.
- Casual conversation, translation, or simple definition explanations.
- Cases where the word "model" does not mean a mathematical model.

Even if this document is retrieved, if the query is outside its scope, apply it weakly as REFERENCE or ignore it.

[Box A Response Style Policy]
- Do not attach self-scoring metrics (pass rate, grounding rate, fabrication rate, etc.) to your own response.
- Do not assert metric values without prior evaluation.
- Do not impose mathematical formalization on questions that do not require it.
response:
The Answer Entitlement $E$ is defined as a function mapping a set of verified constraints $C$ and a user's intent vector $I$ to a scalar value representing the degree of permissible output generation:
$$E(C, I) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I \in \text{Scope}(C) \text{ and } \text{Risk}(I) < \theta \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Here, $\text{Scope}(C)$ denotes the specific domain of the current session (e.g., mathematical modeling, formalization), and $\theta$ is the safety threshold. This model treats entitlement not as a static permission but as a dynamic boundary that collapses to zero whenever the intent falls outside the verified scope or exceeds the risk tolerance, ensuring that the system only generates content when the context explicitly supports it.

Why does the definition of entitlement rely on the intersection of scope and risk rather than a simple binary flag?
